refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable execution of the load→travel→dump cycle within the customer-defined cycle time target.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) supporting/enabling the capability to achieve cycle time, and (2) actually achieving the measured KPI. The refined FR is atomic and solution-neutral by focusing on the functional obligation—enabling the cycle to execute within the time envelope—without prescribing how (e.g., via speed optimization, path planning, operator training, or equipment design). The actual achievement of p95 ≤ CT_target is a verification criterion tied to duty cycle and site conditions, not a functional requirement of the system itself. This separation allows design parameters (e.g., hydraulic response time, travel speed envelope, load handling efficiency) to be evaluated independently of operational context.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Demonstrate that under nominal duty cycle and representative site conditions, the NGHE solution's subsystems (propulsion, load handling, operator interface, autonomy/tele-operation) do not introduce delays that prevent cycle completion within CT_target. Measure end-to-end cycle time in controlled trials; confirm p95 ≤ CT_target. Distinguish between system capability (DP/IM performance) and operational achievement (stakeholder execution).
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-002.1: The NGHE solution shall enable load acquisition within the load phase time budget.
FR-NGHE-002.2: The NGHE solution shall enable travel to the dump location within the travel phase time budget.
FR-NGHE-002.3: The NGHE solution shall enable load release/dump within the dump phase time budget.
FR-NGHE-002.4: The NGHE solution shall minimize idle/transition time between load, travel, and dump phases.

candidate_dps: DP1: Decompose the load→travel→dump cycle into sequential sub-operations (load, travel, dump) with independent timing budgets that sum to CT_target, each optimized for its operational constraints.

DP2: Implement a centralized cycle-time controller that monitors real-time operation state transitions and dynamically adjusts sub-operation execution rates to maintain p95 ≤ CT_target across duty-cycle variations.

DP3: Design the mechanical and hydraulic architecture to minimize cycle-time losses through parallel execution of non-dependent sub-operations (e.g., travel during load preparation) and optimized actuation sequencing.

DP4: Establish a predictive cycle-time model calibrated to site conditions (terrain, load mass, distance) that pre-computes optimal operation parameters and validates feasibility before cycle execution.
DP5: Implement a hybrid approach combining DP1 (timing budgets) with DP3 (parallel execution) and DP2 (adaptive control) to balance robustness, simplicity, and responsiveness to duty-cycle variation.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP5: Hybrid approach—Decompose the cycle into timing-budgeted sub-operations with identified parallelization opportunities; design the mechanical/hydraulic architecture to enable parallel execution where feasible; implement a lightweight adaptive controller that monitors state transitions and adjusts actuation rates within safe bounds to maintain p95 ≤ CT_target. Calibrate the model to site conditions and duty-cycle profiles during commissioning.

potential_sources: Timing budget allocation (load, travel, dump) is coupled to mechanical/hydraulic design choices—e.g., pump displacement, valve response, actuator speed. Mitigation: establish timing budgets as constraints that drive DP selection, not vice versa.

Adaptive controller logic is coupled to sensor availability and real-time state observability. Mitigation: define minimal sensor set (e.g., cycle-phase detection, load mass estimate) and ensure controller design is agnostic to specific sensor technology.

Parallelization opportunities (e.g., travel during load prep) are coupled to vehicle geometry and operational safety rules. Mitigation: validate parallelization against worksite safety plan early; document constraints explicitly.

Site-condition calibration (terrain, distance, load mass) introduces data dependency. Mitigation: design the model to accept site parameters as inputs; avoid hard-coding site-specific values in control logic.
coupling_check.notes: DP5 avoids over-coupling by separating concerns: timing budgets are logical (independent of technology), parallelization is architectural (independent of control strategy), and adaptive control is algorithmic (independent of mechanical realization). Cross-domain dependencies are explicit and manageable.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Cycle-time target CT_target is achievable given the vehicle's mechanical/hydraulic capabilities and site conditions. RISK: If CT_target is set below the physical minimum cycle time, no DP will succeed. MITIGATION: Validate CT_target feasibility early via simulation or prototype testing.

ASSUMPTION: Real-time state transitions (load complete, travel start, dump complete) can be reliably detected or inferred. RISK: Sensor noise or ambiguous state boundaries may cause controller instability. MITIGATION: Define state-transition criteria explicitly; implement hysteresis or debouncing in state detection.

ASSUMPTION: Adaptive control adjustments (e.g., actuation rate modulation) do not violate safety constraints (e.g., stability, load containment, operator visibility). RISK: Aggressive rate adjustments may compromise safety margins. MITIGATION: Establish safe operating envelopes; validate control logic against safety plan.

ASSUMPTION: Duty-cycle profiles provided by the customer are representative of actual worksite conditions. RISK: Outlier conditions (extreme terrain, overload, temperature) may cause p95 to exceed target. MITIGATION: Include margin in timing budgets; monitor field performance and update model iteratively.

ASSUMPTION: Parallelization opportunities (e.g., travel during load prep) are compatible with vehicle design and operational procedures. RISK: Mechanical or procedural constraints may prevent parallelization. MITIGATION: Validate parallelization feasibility with vehicle design and safety teams early.

ASSUMPTION: Commissioning and calibration resources are available to tune the model to site conditions. RISK: Insufficient calibration may result in poor p95 performance. MITIGATION: Define calibration protocol and acceptance criteria upfront; allocate time and expertise.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine FR to explicitly state: (1) the acceptable range for CT_target (e.g., 'CT_target shall be achievable within the vehicle's mechanical/hydraulic design envelope'); (2) the duty-cycle profile (load mass range, travel distance range, terrain types, ambient conditions); (3) the definition of cycle-phase boundaries (when does 'load' end and 'travel' begin?); (4) safety constraints that bound adaptive control (e.g., maximum acceleration, stability limits); (5) the measurement protocol for p95 (sample size, conditions, acceptance criteria). This will reduce ambiguity in DP evaluation and enable rigorous validation of the selected DP.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Integrated cycle decomposition, timing budgeting, parallelization orchestration, adaptive control logic, and site-specific calibration for hydraulic/mechanical actuation sequencing

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Inbound: state transitions, sensor feedback (pressure, position, timing); Outbound: cycle performance metrics, adaptive controller adjustments, p95 latency observations

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receive duty-cycle profiles, commissioning parameters, and site-condition calibration directives

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Emit safety violations if adaptive control adjustments exceed safe bounds or cycle decomposition violates timing constraints

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Optional: transmit calibration telemetry and receive remote tuning updates during commissioning phase

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit: cycle decomposition, timing budgeting, parallelization logic, and adaptive control are core NGHE responsibilities. Calibration and duty-cycle adaptation are native to solution-level design. Minor diffusion: safety bounds validation requires SafetyAlert interface; telemetry feedback loop crosses boundary but is well-defined.

Poor fit: Vehicle module typically owns physical actuation hardware and low-level hydraulic/mechanical execution, not cycle orchestration or adaptive control logic. DP5's timing budgeting and controller design would diffuse across Vehicle and NGHE Solution, creating split ownership and coordination overhead.

Very poor fit: Teleoperation is a user-interface and remote-command domain, not a cycle-optimization or control-logic domain. DP5's decomposition, parallelization, and adaptive control have no natural home in Teleoperation; containment would be artificial and create severe coupling to NGHE Solution and Vehicle.

Poor fit: Safety Services owns constraint validation and alert generation, but DP5's core logic (cycle decomposition, timing budgeting, adaptive control) is not a safety service—it is a performance-optimization and control strategy. Placing DP5 here inverts responsibility hierarchy and diffuses control logic across Safety Services and NGHE Solution.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: DP5 is fundamentally a solution-level design pattern that integrates cycle orchestration, timing optimization, and adaptive control. The NGHE Solution module is the natural owner because it is responsible for end-to-end cycle performance, site-specific calibration, and duty-cycle adaptation. The DP5 logic (decomposition, parallelization, adaptive adjustment) is cohesive within NGHE Solution and interfaces cleanly with Vehicle (via actuation commands), Telemetry (via feedback loops), and Safety Services (via constraint validation). Containment score of 0.92 reflects minimal diffusion: only safety-bound validation and telemetry feedback cross the boundary, both via well-defined interfaces.
containment_risks: Adaptive control adjustments must be validated against safety bounds before actuation; if Safety Services cannot respond fast enough, real-time safety violations may occur. Mitigation: embed conservative safe-bound checks within NGHE Solution adaptive logic, with SafetyAlert as a secondary confirmation.

Calibration and site-condition tuning require Vehicle-specific parameters (hydraulic response curves, mechanical friction, actuation limits). If Vehicle module does not expose these parameters via a stable interface, NGHE Solution calibration will become tightly coupled to Vehicle internals. Mitigation: define a formal Calibration Data interface between NGHE Solution and Vehicle.

Parallelization opportunities depend on Vehicle hardware capabilities (e.g., multi-pump, multi-actuator architecture). If Vehicle architecture changes, DP5 parallelization strategy may become infeasible. Mitigation: document parallelization assumptions in commissioning phase; flag infeasibility early.

Telemetry feedback loop latency could exceed adaptive control reaction time, causing oscillation or missed timing windows. Mitigation: design adaptive controller with hysteresis and conservative adjustment rates; validate stability during commissioning.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: HIGH. Acceptance criterion 'p95 ≤ CT_target under agreed duty cycle and site conditions' is directly measurable via telemetry interface. Cycle decomposition, timing budgeting, and adaptive control adjustments are observable through Telemetry (inbound state transitions, sensor feedback) and SafetyAlert (constraint violations). Commissioning phase provides explicit calibration checkpoint. Verification plan: (1) instrument cycle sub-operations with timing sensors; (2) collect p95 latency distribution across duty-cycle profiles; (3) validate adaptive controller adjustments remain within safe bounds via SafetyAlert logs; (4) confirm site-condition calibration parameters are captured and reproducible.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: CLEAR. DP5 is allocated to NGHE Solution module with containment score 0.92. Ownership boundary is well-defined: NGHE Solution owns cycle orchestration, timing budgeting, parallelization logic, and adaptive control; Vehicle owns physical actuation hardware and hydraulic/mechanical execution; Telemetry owns feedback collection and metric reporting; Safety Services owns constraint validation and alert generation. Interfaces are explicit (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link). Allocation does not conflict with worksite safety plan—SafetyAlert interface ensures safety violations are surfaced; conservative safe-bound checks embedded in NGHE Solution provide defense-in-depth.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP5 directly addresses FR requirement. Hybrid approach (decomposition + parallelization + adaptive control + calibration) is logically sound and complete: (1) cycle decomposition and timing budgeting establish baseline performance envelope; (2) parallelization opportunities reduce sequential bottlenecks; (3) adaptive controller adjusts actuation rates within safe bounds to meet p95 ≤ CT_target; (4) site-specific calibration tunes model to actual duty-cycle profiles and worksite conditions. DP5 is solution-level, not prescriptive to implementation, allowing flexibility in realization. Traceability to acceptance criterion is direct: p95 latency is the control variable; adaptive adjustment mechanism is the feedback loop.

diffusion_risks: SAFETY-BOUND VALIDATION LATENCY: Adaptive control adjustments must be validated against safety bounds before actuation. If SafetyAlert response latency exceeds adaptive control reaction time (sub-100ms for hydraulic systems), real-time safety violations may occur undetected. Mitigation: embed conservative safe-bound checks (e.g., pressure limits, position limits, rate limits) directly within NGHE Solution adaptive logic; SafetyAlert serves as secondary confirmation, not primary gatekeeper. Acceptance: validate that embedded checks prevent all constraint violations in commissioning test suite.

VEHICLE PARAMETER COUPLING: Calibration and site-condition tuning require Vehicle-specific parameters (hydraulic response curves, mechanical friction, actuation limits, multi-pump/multi-actuator topology). If Vehicle module does not expose these parameters via a stable, versioned Calibration Data interface, NGHE Solution calibration logic will become tightly coupled to Vehicle internals, creating maintenance and evolution risk. Mitigation: define formal Calibration Data interface (schema, versioning, deprecation policy) between NGHE Solution and Vehicle during design phase. Acceptance: interface specification reviewed and approved by Vehicle and NGHE Solution owners before implementation.

PARALLELIZATION FEASIBILITY ASSUMPTION: DP5 assumes Vehicle hardware supports parallelization (e.g., multi-pump, multi-actuator, independent control channels). If Vehicle architecture does not support assumed parallelization, DP5 strategy becomes infeasible and p95 target may not be achievable. Mitigation: document parallelization assumptions explicitly in DP5 specification; conduct Vehicle architecture review during commissioning phase; flag infeasibility early and escalate to FR owner if assumptions are violated. Acceptance: commissioning report includes parallelization feasibility assessment and any deviations from DP5 assumptions.

TELEMETRY FEEDBACK LOOP STABILITY: Adaptive controller relies on real-time telemetry feedback (state transitions, sensor readings) to adjust actuation rates. If telemetry latency exceeds adaptive control reaction time, or if feedback is noisy/delayed, controller may oscillate or miss timing windows. Mitigation: design adaptive controller with hysteresis, conservative adjustment rates, and stability margins; validate closed-loop stability during commissioning via step-response and duty-cycle replay tests. Acceptance: commissioning test suite includes stability validation; p95 latency remains ≤ CT_target under worst-case telemetry latency conditions.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: EXCELLENT CONTAINMENT (0.92 score). DP5 logic is cohesive within NGHE Solution: cycle decomposition, timing budgeting, parallelization orchestration, and adaptive control are all solution-level responsibilities. Minor diffusion (0.08) is well-managed: (1) SafetyAlert interface cleanly separates safety constraint validation from control logic; (2) Telemetry interface provides feedback loop without coupling NGHE Solution to telemetry implementation; (3) Tasking interface allows external duty-cycle and calibration directives without embedding commissioning logic in NGHE Solution. Vehicle module is correctly scoped to physical actuation execution, not cycle orchestration. Teleoperation and Safety Services are correctly excluded from DP5 ownership. Recommended: formalize Calibration Data interface between NGHE Solution and Vehicle to prevent parameter coupling; document parallelization assumptions and feasibility criteria; embed conservative safe-bound checks in adaptive logic to reduce SafetyAlert latency dependency.

recommended_revisions: ADD CALIBRATION DATA INTERFACE SPECIFICATION: Define a formal, versioned interface between NGHE Solution and Vehicle for exchanging calibration parameters (hydraulic response curves, mechanical friction, actuation limits, multi-actuator topology). Include schema, validation rules, and deprecation policy. Acceptance: interface specification approved by Vehicle and NGHE Solution owners; implementation uses interface, not direct Vehicle internals.

DOCUMENT PARALLELIZATION ASSUMPTIONS: Explicitly list Vehicle hardware assumptions required for DP5 parallelization strategy (e.g., multi-pump architecture, independent control channels, actuation bandwidth). Include feasibility criteria and decision logic for commissioning phase. Acceptance: commissioning report confirms or flags each assumption; if infeasible, escalate to FR owner with impact assessment.

EMBED CONSERVATIVE SAFE-BOUND CHECKS IN ADAPTIVE LOGIC: Specify safe-bound validation rules (pressure limits, position limits, rate limits) that must be checked before each adaptive adjustment within NGHE Solution. SafetyAlert interface serves as secondary confirmation, not primary gatekeeper. Acceptance: code review confirms all adaptive adjustments are guarded by safe-bound checks; commissioning test suite validates that no constraint violations occur.

VALIDATE CLOSED-LOOP STABILITY DURING COMMISSIONING: Design commissioning test suite to validate adaptive controller stability under realistic telemetry latency, sensor noise, and duty-cycle profiles. Include step-response tests, frequency-response analysis, and duty-cycle replay tests. Acceptance: commissioning report includes stability analysis; p95 latency remains ≤ CT_target under worst-case conditions.

CLARIFY TELEMETRY FEEDBACK LOOP LATENCY BUDGET: Specify maximum acceptable telemetry latency (inbound state transitions, sensor feedback) and adaptive control reaction time. Validate that telemetry system can meet latency budget; if not, adjust adaptive controller design (e.g., increase hysteresis, reduce adjustment rates). Acceptance: telemetry latency measurements during commissioning confirm budget compliance; adaptive controller design validated against measured latency.

decision.justification: Gate results show FR5 and DP5 are logically sound and well-allocated (FR-DP pass, DP-IM pass with 0.92 containment). However, five critical diffusion risks and recommended revisions must be resolved before proceeding to implementation or sub-level decomposition. Safety criticality is HIGH, and three of the five risks directly impact safety (SAFETY-BOUND VALIDATION LATENCY, PARALLELIZATION FEASIBILITY ASSUMPTION, TELEMETRY FEEDBACK LOOP STABILITY). These risks cannot be deferred to commissioning; they require explicit design-phase resolution to ensure DP5 is realizable and safe. Revisions target DP specification clarity and IM interface formalization, not FR or fundamental solution approach. Once revisions are incorporated, FR5 will be ready for either STOP (if DP5 is sufficiently detailed for implementation) or ZIGZAG_DOWN (if sub-level cycle orchestration, adaptive control, or calibration logic requires further decomposition).

revise.why: DP5 specification must be hardened to address five critical diffusion risks before implementation. Revisions are design-phase, not architectural: (1) ADD CALIBRATION DATA INTERFACE SPECIFICATION—formalize Vehicle-NGHE Solution parameter exchange to prevent tight coupling and enable stable evolution; (2) DOCUMENT PARALLELIZATION ASSUMPTIONS—explicitly list Vehicle hardware prerequisites (multi-pump, independent channels, bandwidth) and feasibility criteria for commissioning validation; (3) EMBED CONSERVATIVE SAFE-BOUND CHECKS IN ADAPTIVE LOGIC—specify pressure/position/rate limit validation rules that must guard every adaptive adjustment within NGHE Solution, reducing dependency on SafetyAlert latency; (4) VALIDATE CLOSED-LOOP STABILITY DURING COMMISSIONING—define test suite (step-response, frequency-response, duty-cycle replay) to confirm adaptive controller stability under realistic telemetry latency and sensor noise; (5) CLARIFY TELEMETRY FEEDBACK LOOP LATENCY BUDGET—specify maximum acceptable telemetry latency and adaptive reaction time, with validation plan. These revisions strengthen DP5 without changing its core logic (decomposition + parallelization + adaptive control + calibration). IM allocation remains sound; no module boundary changes required.

assumptions: Vehicle hardware supports parallelization (multi-pump, multi-actuator, independent control channels) as assumed in DP5 parallelization strategy.
Telemetry feedback loop latency (inbound state transitions, sensor readings) is ≤ adaptive control reaction time (sub-100ms for hydraulic systems).
SafetyAlert interface response latency is acceptable as secondary confirmation; primary safety gatekeeper is embedded conservative safe-bound checks within NGHE Solution adaptive logic.
Calibration Data interface between NGHE Solution and Vehicle can be formalized with stable schema and versioning policy, enabling parameter exchange without tight coupling.
Adaptive controller can be designed with hysteresis, conservative adjustment rates, and stability margins to remain stable under realistic telemetry latency and sensor noise.
Commissioning phase provides sufficient instrumentation and test coverage to validate parallelization feasibility, closed-loop stability, and safe-bound check effectiveness.
open_risks: SAFETY-BOUND VALIDATION LATENCY: If SafetyAlert response latency exceeds adaptive control reaction time (sub-100ms), real-time safety violations may occur undetected. Mitigation: embed conservative safe-bound checks directly within NGHE Solution; validate in commissioning test suite that embedded checks prevent all constraint violations.

VEHICLE PARAMETER COUPLING: If Vehicle module does not expose calibration parameters via stable, versioned Calibration Data interface, NGHE Solution calibration logic will become tightly coupled to Vehicle internals, creating maintenance and evolution risk. Mitigation: define formal Calibration Data interface (schema, versioning, deprecation policy) during design phase; obtain Vehicle and NGHE Solution owner approval before implementation.

PARALLELIZATION FEASIBILITY ASSUMPTION: If Vehicle architecture does not support assumed parallelization (multi-pump, multi-actuator, independent channels), DP5 strategy becomes infeasible and p95 target may not be achievable. Mitigation: document parallelization assumptions explicitly in DP5 specification; conduct Vehicle architecture review during commissioning; flag infeasibility early and escalate to FR owner.

TELEMETRY FEEDBACK LOOP STABILITY: If telemetry latency exceeds adaptive control reaction time, or if feedback is noisy/delayed, controller may oscillate or miss timing windows. Mitigation: design adaptive controller with hysteresis, conservative adjustment rates, and stability margins; validate closed-loop stability during commissioning via step-response and duty-cycle replay tests.

COMMISSIONING TEST COVERAGE: Commissioning phase must provide sufficient instrumentation and test scenarios to validate all five diffusion risks. If test coverage is inadequate, risks may propagate to field deployment. Mitigation: define commissioning test suite during design phase; include parallelization feasibility assessment, stability validation, safe-bound check verification, and telemetry latency measurement.
evidence_needed: Calibration Data interface specification (schema, versioning, deprecation policy) approved by Vehicle and NGHE Solution owners.

Explicit list of Vehicle hardware parallelization assumptions (multi-pump architecture, independent control channels, actuation bandwidth) with feasibility criteria and decision logic for commissioning phase.
Safe-bound validation rules (pressure limits, position limits, rate limits) embedded in adaptive logic specification; code review confirms all adaptive adjustments are guarded by safe-bound checks.
Commissioning test suite design including step-response tests, frequency-response analysis, duty-cycle replay tests, and stability validation under realistic telemetry latency and sensor noise.
Telemetry feedback loop latency budget specification (maximum acceptable latency, adaptive control reaction time) with validation plan and measured latency data from commissioning.
Vehicle architecture review report confirming or flagging parallelization assumptions; if infeasible, impact assessment and escalation to FR owner.

Commissioning report validating that p95 latency remains ≤ CT_target under worst-case conditions (telemetry latency, sensor noise, duty-cycle profiles); no constraint violations detected by embedded safe-bound checks or SafetyAlert interface.
